sentences.text: Right.
She sent me a few-- a couple-- several times through this young man, our neighbor, who the mother was able to send through someone to him.
And the same time, my mom ask her to also send-- send at the same time a package for me.
So was pillows, and warm feather blankets so I keep warm, clothes from home what I wanted.
And between the same thing, the last package was, she had some material from the beginning of the war, some chiffon.
And it was like la-- like satin-- one striped satin and one chiffon.
And she made up this-- I don't know how she managed.
She made in ghetto-- at that time, she was already in ghetto, [?
Sradula, ?] where they concentrate all the Jews.
Whatever surrounding, the last Jews whatever remain, they sent them to [?
Stradula ?], to the ghetto, before they liquidate.
So mom was able to-- I don't know how she managed.
She made me summer shoes in the last package.
And this mat-- she made just a blouse from this whole material, which was-- which she had.
And this supposed to be for the sweet 16.
And she didn't have anything else, so she sent me, with the blouse-- which I lost the blouse by moving-- she sent it with her own little lace blouse-- I mean, little short sleeve shirt.
So I wear that under that blouse.
And that was actually your mother's?
And that was that, from my mom, yes.
That was the last package.

That's why-- miraculously, I don't know how this survived, being so many-- from Germany, through the-- going through all the hell I went through, to Peterswaldau, and all the travels, and went around from Toledo, to here, and from one place to another, that this didn't get lost.
Everything else did.
But this, I-- somehow I have it.
It's like a miracle, just maybe because I love it so much, and it's so dear, just to be remembered that my mom-- she-- to live in my heart.
And this what she is-- maybe for a future generation to remember, to know that it was true.
So that's what she sent me.
And-- yes, I'm sorry.
I was hide-- matter of fact, I hide it also-- I hid the picture of my mom, which I had, that I made it from the-- and we'd been-- later on, we'd been in shop.
And they would send us out.
I had a purse.
And I put it in between.
And then I had a little golden ring for my birthday.
Mom gave me a golden little heart, with my monogram.
So I kept it at that time, too.
They didn't take everything away then.
And that was in men's camp, yeah, the Markstadt.
Later on, they took everything away.
This, everything, I hid under the [GERMAN], what they call straw, and the-- but let me get first to it.
They took gradually.
Then they took away some clothes, but I still had some from home.
And everybody shared with me.
And my daddy-- then, they took away-- my daddy worked in the worst [GERMAN] at the [GERMAN] in Markstadt.
And then they took away from him the warm boots.
And he had a warm jacket with fur underneath.
They took away this from him.
And he worked in [? water, ?] in very difficult conditions.
And he got pneumonia.
He got ill.
And at that time, I tried to help whatever I could.
It was very serious.
And that time also, there was an order that SS is taking over.
And they need 60 girls to send to a-- somewhere else.
I didn't know where.
But there were-- nobody knew.
After, we found out it was Peterswaldau.
That was a ladies' camp, which-- but momentarily, nobody knew what.
They made an [? appel. ?] And they came, the whole 120 girls, they were on the-- once-- they formed [? appel ?] and standing.
They were counting.
And then came the Germans, [GERMAN] they were called.
And he looked over the whole-- made a parade, looking out everybody who to select, who not to select.
Most of them he'd-- seems like he'd select the younger girls.
And I tried-- knowing then daddy was ill, I tried to disguise myself.
So I put on my little scarf over my head and tried to hide behind some people, which I succeeded for time being.
I wasn't selected between the 60 girls.
But sure enough, somebody was very nice.
And they-- uh, how you say?
Told on me-- how you say this in there?
Can you help me out?
It's-- they-- [SIGHS] They told--
They-- somebody gave out my name, that I was-- that I fooled, that I disgui-- how you say?
The--
Yeah, turned me in.
And they made a special-- there is another word for it that I forgot.
The-- I was-- maybe later it will come back to me.
Anyway, they made a special [? appel ?] again.
And the [GERMAN] came out and he says he was looking for me.
And he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means, where is the little brunette?
I he says, [GERMAN].
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
That I don't want literally to translate exactly what that meant, but it was pretty bad.
He said, you fooled the [GERMAN], which was the SS.
And that was a bad-- something to-- against-- you know, to fool somebody in that kind of higher rank, especially the SS, that was something punishable.
I expected-- I was lucky they didn't send me-- lucky-- it was surprising they didn't send me to Auschwitz for it.
Because that was something-- I went and I did something, which should have never-- somebody-- it wasn't a collaborator.
What is it called?
It's a-- well, you know what I mean, that somebody--
You were hiding yourself.
And they--
Yes, but this is somebody gave me out from this.
How you say it in the-- there is another.
They turned me in.
How do you say this?
There's another word for that.
Momentarily, I'm too nervous now.
And he did not.
I wasn't sent to Auschwitz, but they did send me away to Peterswalsdau.
That's--
With the 60 girls.
And when I came, before I part with my daddy-- daddy was ill.
That was the last time I saw my daddy.
I came to the [GERMAN].
And daddy managed to walk done from this bridge, from the-- from this bunk.
He had pneumonia then.
I tried to hold on, as long as I could, to be some help to my daddy.
Why I did it?
Because no matter how I knew that was a very drastic step what I made.
But to me, the thought that I can be a few-- whatever days I can be with my dad, try to help him when he was so ill, so that's what gave me this idea to do it.
But when daddy came down, and he said-- before we part, he looked at me, and he said, remember there is a God and be a Jew.
That was his last words when we part.
[CRYING]
After that, I went to Mak-- to Peterswaldau.
And then, the other, a few weeks later, there was another transport came, the other 60.
And some other people came.
And we were working for the-- for the-- in a big factory for ammunition.
I was sent-- I was-- I wasn't.
I mean, that was just-- coincidentally, they did not assign me to heavy physical work.
I was in a-- we still, we wear our own clothes.
They didn't cut the hair then.
And I was in a very-- comparing with the other departments, I was-- they-- I just worked on those little-- like little, for the bombs, what you like, for the watches, for the time?
What they call this, gears?
No.
Gears within the watch?
Little tiny-- yeah, with the tiny little, with the like-- and I had with the-- I had the magnifying glass in my eye.
And I had to control it, to check every little one, which was very strenuous for my vision.
Yes, it was ammunition, but there were a lot of separate, different kinds of parts to make for the bombs.
I had [INAUDIBLE] I had this precise work, which was very tiny, which was not very difficult, but you have to strain your eyes, naturally.
And which I lost-- my vision got so weak.
And I made a lot of [GERMAN], they called it.
But [INAUDIBLE] surprised they didn't do nothing to me.
But they sent me-- me and 10-- nine other girls-- in the cattle wagons, what they call.
And we were treated horrifically.
We were treated like a-- you know, no dignity, no human feeling.
We were just treated like worse than cattle.
I envy-- I envied a bird.
I envied anything-- you know, young girls-- not only myself-- but all the girls were in-- young girl were being there also.
And we all just looked outside and said, we-- I wish we could be-- I think no one realized the-- but to be free, or to walk on the streets, or to breathe free, to be a human being.
And then I came to-- they sent us to Breslau, to a optician.
And they gave us the glasses.
And he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
He gave me glasses.
I left it on the machine.
But it did help me to work easier with no-- I'm surprised, like I said, they didn't send me to-- so again--
They sent us back.
They sent us, surely.
Because we-- they need our-- they need the working.
They need the work.
And we did-- been good workers.
That's why they went-- made the effort.
Otherwise, they wouldn't bother.
They could have sent us right to Auschwitz.
But they needed help.
And we did the work well.
So that's why they sent to us to the factory-- and I mean to the optician, to help them.
Peterswaldau was near the Bayern.
Near the Bayern.
That's in the lower part of Germany.
And then the SS took over.
When the SS came, we had to-- we had a order to be all undressed, all nude like God-- our-- completely.
And they took most of the clothes away.
And they gave all the everything what one has, has to give up.
And we have to be nude.
And they were sitting-- I never forget.
We were sitting a big table, two or three.
I didn't remember anymore, it's been such a long time.
But we're all standing.
And then we have to turn around, so they can see us from the front and from the back, for any defects or whatever.
And they will select who to live-- who gets the number, who doesn't get the number.
I got the number 26-8-11.
I never forget this number.
And you know, funny how a child gets the idea, how I summarized all those numbers.
I put them all together. 1 and 1 is 2.
And 8 is 10.
And 6 and 2 is 8.
That means 18. 18 in Hebrew is 'chai', to live.
And I, for some reason, and this is-- it's how ridiculous it sounds, but this gave me, for some reason, some hope that I might be alive-- live through.
We all live with some hope.
That's what give us some strength.
And the same time, I also had this little golden ring.
And I say, if they going to look at my-- I say, if they going to-- I'm going to take it in my mouth.
If they're going to look at my mouth, I'm going to swallow.
That's how naive people are.
It's funny how you think-- you know, kids don't even realize.
Teenagers-- they don't realize the danger.
I could have choked, too.
But I did it.
And it happened that I-- they didn't look in my mouth.
And I took out after, and I sewed up in my little bra.
And I had this little ring after the war, until '48.
But obviously, from wearing out and washing, I-- it broke.
And my husband took-- at that time, I was ill-- and took-- I was in hospital.
So he took this and put it in his back pocket.
And obviously, when he pulled out, he forgot, and it was gone.
Being in Peterswaldau, when we arrive and the SS took over-- [SIGHS] now this is a-- this is something.
They gave us-- [SIGHS] they give us soap to wash, green bars.
It was a greenish bar-- smelt horrible.
It wasn't even making any soap, really.
[INAUDIBLE], but there wasn't no soap.
It says, the lettering, RYF.
That means [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means Pure Jewish Fat.
That was made from human, from our people.
That's the soap they gave us.
[CRYING] Excuse me.
[CRYING] I remained-- I remained there.
I took my-- some clothes.
And I hid under the straw, [INAUDIBLE] there was straw and burlap wrap, sort of.
And I was able to even to have the pillow with my mom's, and me, and all this-- feather blanket, and some clothes-- not all of them.
Some I left at the wall-- my raincoat, my other clothes, my summer dresses, and the picture.
Yeah.
It was '44 when they-- then I heard, in '43, after the war, somebody in Israel, which was our neighbor, but her parents were the same with us, for the liquidation.
They sent the whole trans-- the whole transport.
She was in Auschwitz.
After she escaped from Auschwitz, she was in music.
They tried, you know to-- they played while the people are screaming, the gas chambers.
So she was in music after she escaped from there.
And she was on the underground.
And when I met her in Israel, so she told me that was the last transport in August, '43, when they liquidate the ghetto.
When they liquidate in the ghetto in '43, they sent all the Jews-- they didn't have time to segregate.
I was hoping still maybe, somebody, someone, somehow.
My sister was so gorgeous, so young, youthful and full of life.
My mom, my cousins-- nobody.
They took the whole transport away, straight to the-- straight to the gas chamber.
Nobody survived.
I wanted to say something else about happened in Peterswaldau.
Yeah.
And one of the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means group, from the group Jewish girls were-- there were certain group what they had one on the top, I mean as the head group, the leader of the group.
And the main of the-- there was 1,200 women and girls in this camp, Peterswaldau.
Later they brought in from different camps to this camp, because they need, obviously, work to be done, help.
[SIGHS] And she was Branca Weinstock.
She was the-- a blonde girl.
She was very energetic.
And she was-- and first of all, the [GERMAN], the SS, the woman-- but she was in charge of all the Jews first.
If anything, she had first to go to her, and whatever.
There was always-- she was in charge of the Jewish girls.
So one of the group girls-- she approached me.
And she asked me-- her name was [?
Gutka, ?] but I don't remember.
There were two sisters, Gutka and the other one I don't remember.
Maybe later, when I-- anyway, she asked me to make a biography.
She knew I was into drawing with a pencil.
And actually, we had none.
She brought me some little card, about that size.
And she said she was going to-- she asked me to make the biography, the whole how we arrived, how it happened, how we-- the [NON-ENGLISH], how they counted.
They counted four in a row.
And the whole camp was on one-- twice a day they used to count us, so somebody didn't escape or whatever.
And she told me she-- after how it was the SS took us over, the whole picture.
So we had to describe the [NON-ENGLISH], and the SS, how they took us over.
And then she told me that she was going to put this in a letter and give us a gift to the [NON-ENGLISH] this, to the Branca Weinstock.
A gift to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the head of the Jew-- of the Jews.
She was Branca Weinstein-- Weinstock.
As a matter of fact, I-- she lives now in Israel, in Tel Aviv.
And I called her when I was there, but she wasn't home.
Her daughter answered the phone.
She was in London.
I didn't say why.
I wanted my kids-- they said-- now I am impossible for me to be able to concentrate, and to bring all this back life, the way it was, to reproduce the same thing.
So it was difficult for me.
So I said, maybe if she would be nice enough, she'd still had it, to make a copy for the children to show, for my grandchildren.
But she never respond.
I wrote a letter to her and she never respond to it.
Why did she asked you to write all this down?

She wanted to have the biography what we went through-- how it will look, how we marched to work with the-- with the SS women to work, and how it will look, how we visualize after the war, how we're going to be free people, be able to be again human being, and walk on the streets-- in the sidewalks, rather than in the streets, and like we'd been walking and marching.
There we can be breathing free human being again.
I think this she wanted-- I didn't sign my name, no-- there was anonymously.
I just made the picture with the pen-- with a pencil, on the-- and I used to draw.
Some other people asked me, so I did some drawing.
[SIGHS] And that was it.
And I did not-- that was after that.
And then they sent a transport from Auschwitz, some women--
To Peterswaldau.
I am-- this is almost by the end-- the big-- I can recall, the beginning of '45, or '44, by the end of '44.
I cannot recall.
It's very difficult for me.
No.
They sent a group from Auschwitz to us--
--from they were Hungarian women.
And they were no hair and they were completely shaved off.
And the clothes-- they were like sacks, were terrible, gray.
And they were so frightened.
They walked by the wall.
They didn't know where they were.
Who-- anyway, one of them worked with me, by the same table.
She was-- reminded me so much of my mom, the blue eyes.
But she was with her niece, Magda.
She was from Budapest.
And it happened that she liked me very much.
We happened-- somehow she cared for me.
And when the war ended, '45 in May-- the 5th was the liberation day.
Some of them-- excuse me, before I say it, they started to liquidate our camp.
That was the last march, like they talked-- I didn't know what was going on.
Yeah, and for this what I made the drawing-- the most important thing-- for the drawing, I receive a bread.
But I don't know if anyone can comprehend this, what the bread meant in camp, in concentration camp.
As much as I didn't want to eat at home, but here was a dream.
If I ever-- there wasn't really bread.
It was more like a-- like a sawdust, or made out of some-- a heavy dough-- dough.
It's not even dough.
What was it?
Like a lime.
But to me, it was most delicious thing I could ever dream of at that time.
And I got the whole bread-- imagine this-- for myself.
Who was the drawing for?
Sorry?
I told you.
The-- [?
Gutka. ?] She was a foreman-- she wasn't a foreman.
She was like for the group [INAUDIBLE].
But she was a different department, not in mine.
But they knew me from singing, from drawing.
So she came and ask me if I could do the drawing for the [NONE-ENGLISH], not for her.
It's for the head of the Jews, of the whole Jewish women, which there was Branca Weinstock.
Weinstock-- I think that's what the name is.
Weintrunk-- it couldn't be Weintrunk.
Weinstock-- I have it written down, the address and telephone.
And she gave it to her, as a mem-- or something to-- like a biography from our camp.
I had to visualize her.
I didn't have her in front of me to do.
I have to visualize how she looked, you know, in person.
But I later on tried to close my eyes and to put this on-- on this little paper, which came out perfect, by the way.
I could never do this again.
She was blonde.
And she had the braids taken in front-- very attractive, with the gray jacket, made out of blanket-- who knows what.
And she had a brown skirt, I think, pleated.
She was-- you know, she was the one who was in charge of this camp.
Beside what and above her was the German SS women, also.
But she was-- she was a witch.
She was horrible.
She wanted-- matter of fact, she wanted to liquidate the camp.
That was already rumored.
They started-- they took, I think, 170 girls for the death march, which I didn't know.
They took 170 girls out of our concentration camp.
And I don't-- God know what happened to them.
And then, the last minute, the [NON-ENGLISH] said-- yeah, Branca Weinstock.
She went to the SS woman and pleaded with her to leave us in camp, because the end was so close, obviously, that she persuade her.
And she did-- left us in camp, [? without ?] evacuation anymore.
And that's why we remain in Peterswaldau.
Yeah, Peterswaldau-- I just said.
Then they left us there, without moving-- nowhere, with the liquation.
Otherwise, we wouldn't be here.
And--
Russia.
May the 5th, the Russian marched in and they liberated us.
And they Hungar-- I told you about the lady who I met from Hungary, Hungarian lady?
Her husband was a district doctor-- matter of fact-- from [INAUDIBLE].
When she-- when the war got ended, they sent Red Cross special buses to pick up their citizens.
So she asked me to come with her.
She said, Rosika-- she called me Rosika.
Rosika, please come with me-- the Magda, the niece also.
I said, look, I like you very much, but I had a home and parents who they love me.
And I have-- if I don't find them, someone, then I promise I'll come to you.
She said, remember.
She got tears and crying.
She didn't want to leave-- please, the bus is waiting.
Come, come.
I said, no way.
I have-- I had a home, which I love my parents, they loved me, and I wish they would be alive.
Anyway, so I didn't want to leave.
And she told me to remember at least the name.
It's Dr.
Fried.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I never forget this place where they lived.
I heard the husband did survive.
Anyway--
I went home-- sorry?
So after the liberation, for a few weeks, I remained with two ladies which we'd been-- they were older than I was.
She was 37.
But I cling always to the people, because I need mama.
I think it was the turn away from the parents, so suddenly, I think.
I needed this warmth.
And she needed, because they had small children, I think-- one of them.
She was a wonderful person.
And she wanted-- she stayed with me.
[INAUDIBLE] she has a sister in there, which she didn't go to the sister, she stayed with me.
And we stayed after the war together in Peterswaldau.
And then, after a few weeks, decide to go home.
I went back.
There was no transportation because-- we couldn't get no food.
I went into the camp and get some potatoes, something to get to eat.
And we traveled.
It was-- most of the time, there was no connection.
I went for the first time back home.
To Sosnowiec, where I came from, to Kielce.
It was on a Sunday.
[SIGHS] Outside looked-- the store, with the same lock, the way I was used to seeing at home.
And I went-- I see that emptiness-- no friends, no relatives, no familiar face, completely silent.
